paragraphs.text: Hello, I ask that you please consider looking into the "stop killing games" Initiative when revising the Digital Fairness Act. It aims to protect the consumer from anti-consumer behavior committed by certain actors in the game industry. The industry employs and are moving to use more hostile actions to prevent consumers from accessing and using a digital product they have purchased with no recourse or compensation. I believe this is akin planned obsolescence that the industry is attempting to skirt. They release a product, offer no end date in the scope of release and when they deem it not profitable they render the product in inoperable. Some companies have revised there EULA's to try legally protect themselves by stating they "stop supporting at anytime" (sic) thus rendering the user purchase void and without a tangible good or service after the transaction and without a reasonable notification period. Look at Ubisoft's recent behavior towards their games as an example of the predatory and hostile behavior towards its customers and user base. The Initiative is not asking for every product currently to be saved but moving forward that regulations be put in place to treat the consumer fairly and honour their purchase by creating a means for the product to exist and function in a state equal to merchantable quality.
